oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Cute and Colorful Kakarikis
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Batt, Karen
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This item originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 51 Number 2 (April/May 2024).The following is an excerpt from the full PDF link.The Kakariki (New Zealand parakeet) is not well known, but popularity of these small parrots is growing fast. Small enough for tiny home living or traveling, yet they have large bird personalities accompaniedby pleasant voices and calls. Main species: The most commonly kept in the USA are the red crowned, Cyanoramphus novaezelandiae, with a full red cap, two small red patches on the rump, a streak behind the eyes and blue in the wings.The yellow crowned, C. auriceps, has a red frontal band, but yellow on the crown of the head and no red streak behind the eyes. They are not common, but at times one can see the signs of interbreeding with the red fronted.There is also an Orange fronted, that is endangered. Mutations: The Red crowned come in normal greens, turquoise, cinnamon, and pieds including dark eyed clears (DEC) which are 100% pied. The green based DEC are called goldchecks and the turquoise DEC are creams. There are also spangles, misty, fallows, etc. So the combination of these mutations give a variety of colors.There are no Ino mutations in Kakariki. When the birds are mature the normal and dominant pieds have a reddish colored iris while the recessive pieds have a dark iris. Babies have dark irises. The sex linked cinnamon hatchlings have plum eyes and pinkish legs and feet. The non sex linked fallow have red eyes. A note about the recessive turquoise; they are not blue, they just cannot produce yellow, hence when one has a DEC Turquoise it is cream and not white. The crown and eye stripe of the turquoise are a pale color. Dominantpieds will show up with any variation of a spot on the nape, under the throat or one or a few pied long feathers as well as some pied patterning on the claws, no matter how small an amount. My favorite are the DEC goldchecks. I own a blue eyed cremello mustang horse.Click the PDF link associated with this item to view the complete item as originally published.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 51 No. 2 (2024): Watchbird April/May 2024; 12-14
